content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662         322-5660              322-5901           322-6441              322-6464

April 30, 1982


Steve Weissman
Office of Commissioner Grew
Public Utilities Commission
State Building
350 McAllister Street
San Francisco, CA  94102

Dear Steve:

     This letter is sent to confirm the advice this office
gave you on Monday, April 26, 1982, concerning Commissioner
Priscilla Grew's duties under the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act, Government Code
Sections 87100, et seq.¹/  I am in the midst of preparing a
more detailed written response, but the basic advice is as
follows.

     Rate design proceedings involving Southern California
Gas Company are presently pending before the Public Utilities
Commission.  One of the utility's customers, General Motors,
presented oral arguments at the hearings conducted by an
Administrative Law Judge on the rate design proposals.
Commissioner Grew has an investment of more than $1,000 in
General Motors.

     We concluded that the decisions concerning the utility's
rate design could not have a material financial effect on
General Motors within the meaning of Sections 87100 and
87103.²/  Accordingly, Commissioner Grew is not prohibited

___________________________
     1/  All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise noted.

     2/  The effect on General Motors' rates as a result of
any of the proposals was estimated to be less than 1%.  Thus
the potential effect on General Motors' revenues falls far
short of $100,000, the benchmark used for determining the
materiality of an effect on very large business entities
such as General Motors.  See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18702(b)(1)(A).

Steve Weissman
April 30, 1982
Page Two


from participating in the pending rate design proceedings
involving Southern California Gas Company.

     I also advised you that Commissioner Grew would not be
disqualified under the Act from participating in decisions
that could materially affect the University of California.
Her husband's salary from U.C., even if financed by federal
research grants, is excluded from the definition of "income"
in the Act since it is salary from a government agency.
Section 82030(b)(2). Therefore, U.C. is not a "source of
income" to Commissioner Grew within the meaning of Section
87103.

     I hope to have a more detailed letter to you on this
matter finished by the end of next week. In the meantime,
if I can be of further assistance, please feel free to contact
me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Diane Maura Fishburn
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

DMF:plh

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

May 13, 1982


Steve Weissman
Office of Commissioner Grew
Public Utilities Commission
State Building
350 McAllister Street
San Francisco, CA 94102

Re: Advice Letter
No. A-82-077

Dear Steve:

This letter is sent to follow up on the advice contained in a letter dated April 30, 1982, concerning Commissioner Priscilla Grew's duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act, Government Code Sections 87100, et seq.1/ The advice, as set forth in this letter is provided pursuant to Section 83114(b).

My understanding of the situation is as follows. Priscilla Grew is a member of the California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC). The CPUC regulates the operation of utilities in California. Cal. Pub. Utilities Code Sections 301, et seq. A major and important function of the CPUC in this regard is the approval of all utility rate increases.

Commissioner Grew owns investments worth more than $1,000 in several large companies which do business in California.2/ One of these companies is General Motors

1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted.

2/ These interests have been disclosed on Commissioner Grew's Statement of Economic Interests. Although these interests are held in trust and not directly by Commissioner Grew, it makes no difference for purposes of analyzing whether there is a conflict of interest. Sections 82034, 87103.

Steve Weissman
May 13, 1982
Page 2


Acceptance Corporation, which is wholly owned by General
Motors.

Southern California Gas Company (SoCal Gas) has filed a
request for a rate increase of $800 to $900 million
dollars. Based on a staff proposal, the CPUC decided to
determine the rate design or structure issues prior to
ruling on the rate increase itself. An Administrative Law
Judge has conducted hearings on the rate design issues and
has prepared a draft decision for CPUC consideration.
Commissioner Grew is the "assigned" commissioner for this
matter; my understanding is that this means that she is
responsible for coordinating commission consideration of
the matter. She will play a major role in shaping the
final decision.

During the hearings, two major proposals for SoCal
Gas's rate design were presented. One proposal came from
SoCal Gas itself, and the other came from CPUC staff.
During the hearings, several persons made comments on the
proposals. One of the participants was a representative
from General Motors.

When Commissioner Grew became aware of General Motors'
participation, she requested from you, and you in turn
requested from us, advice as to whether she had a conflict
of interest under the Political Reform Act. We had several
phone discussions regarding this matter during which you
provided me with the following additional information.

SoCal Gas serves approximately 4 million customers in
Southern California. There are 3,663,666 residential
customers, 179,457 commercial customers and 39,681
industrial customers. The current rate structure for SoCal
Gas sets up three tiers for residential rates based upon
the number of therms per month used. Commercial and
industrial customers pay a set amount per therm used which
is equivalent to the second tier residential rate.

Customers are also divided on the basis of peak daily
usage into the following classes: GNL customers are those

Steve Weissman
May 13, 1982
Page 3


who use 100 million cubic feet (mcf) or less (including all
residential users), and GN2 customers are those who use
over 100 mcf.3/ These classifications are used to
determine priority of usage when shortages occur. GN1
customers have the highest priority, and so forth.

The staff proposal for SoCal Gas Co.'s rate design
would set up two tiers for commercial and industrial
customers who are nonresidential GN1 or GN2 users based
upon the number of therms per month used. The price for
the first 300 therms used per month would be $.05 lower
than the rate for all therms used over 300 therms. The
rate for the therms used over 300 would be the same as the
tier 2 residential rate.

In addition to all residential users, GN1 includes
179,065 commercial users and 29,881 industrial users. GN2
has 270 commercial users, and 659 industrial users. A
large number of GN1 customers use under 300 therms per
month. All GN2 customers use over 300 therms per month, and
the majority use over 2,500 therms per month.

Commercial and industrial customers used a total of
6,444,000,000 therms in 1980 which is approximately 60% of
the total number of therms of 10,779,000,000. However,
these customers account for approximately 64% of the
utility's revenue.4/ and they comprise approximately 5.7%
of all customers.

General Motors is a GN2 customer which uses over 2,500
therms per month at its facilities located in Southern
California within SoCal Gas's service area.

ANALYSIS

Government Code Section 87100 provides:

_________________

3/ GN2 customers also cannot use an alternate fuel
to gas. There are other customers (GN3-GN5) who can use
alternate fuels.

4/ Annual sales in 1980 to commercial and industrial
customers totalled $3,327,000,000; total revenues were
$5,212,000,000. 1980 is the most recent year for which
figures are available.

Steve Weissman
May 13, 1982
Page 4


     No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any
way attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason
to know he has a financial interest.

     An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision when it is reasonably foreseeable that there will
be a material financial effect on an investment or interest
in real property of the official, or a source of income to
the official, which is distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally.  Section 87103.  Since Commissioner
Grew has an investment of more than $1,000 in General
Motors, she may not participate in any decision which could
foreseeably have a material financial effect on General
Motors.

     Since it is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed
changes to the utility's rate design will have effects on
General Motors as a ratepayer, the only issues crucial to
the present determination are whether the effect on General
Motors could be material within the meaning of section
87103 and, if so, whether the effect would be
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally.

     The Commission has elaborated on the statutory concept
of materiality in its regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18702 (copy enclosed).  This section provides generally
that a significant effect is material.  2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18702(a).  It also sets forth the following
numerical guidelines to assist in determining the
materiality of an effect on a business entity.

     (1)  Whether, in the case of a business entity in
which the public official holds a direct or indirect
investment of one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more or
in the case of a business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, employee,
trustee or holds any position of management, the effect
of the decision will be to increase or decrease:

     (A)  The annualized gross revenues by the lesser
of:

          1.  One hundred thousand dollars ($100,000); or

          2.  One percent if it is one thousand dollars
($1,000) or more; or

Steve Weissman
May 13, 1982
Page 5


     (B)  Annual net income by the lesser of:

     1.  Fifty thousand dollars ($50,000); or

     2.  One half of one percent if it is one thousand
dollars ($1,000) or more; or

     C.  Current assets or liabilities by the lesser
of:

     1.  One hundred thousand dollars ($100,000); or

     2.  One half of one percent if it is one thousand
dollars ($1,000) or more.

     Current assets are deemed to be decreased by the
amount of any expenses incurred as a result of a
governmental decision.

     Accordingly, in this matter, the decision on the
utility's rate design would have to have a $100,000 effect
on the expenses or gross revenues of General Motors in
order to be material.

     A staff member at the CPUC who worked on the staff
proposal indicated that the effect on General Motors' rates
would be less than 1%, an amount which over a year's period
would clearly fall short of $100,000.5/  Thus, the effect
on General Motors is not material within the meaning of
Section 87103, and Commissioner Grew's participation is not
prohibited.

     Although the effect was determined to not be material,
we also considered the issue of whether these kinds of
decisions fell within the "public generally" exception to
the disqualification statute.  This advice is given
primarily to guide Commissioner Grew in future matters.
The "public generally" exception allows an official to
participate in a decision in which he or she has a

_______________
     5/  We were not able to obtain the exact figures for
General Motors in the short time available to us.  For a 1%
effect to be material or $100,000, GM's annual gas bill
would have to be $10 million dollars or more.  It seems
reasonable to assume, however, that General Motors' annual
gas bill does not exceed $10 million dollars.  If this
proves to be incorrect, then our conclusion would change
accordingly.

Steve Weissman
May 13, 1982
Page 6


financial interest if the decision will affect the public
generally or a significant segment thereof in the same
manner as it affects the official.

As we discussed, there are no hard and fast rules for
determining when a decision has an effect on a business
entity that is distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally. However, there are factors which
influence the determination. First, the size of the group
affected is significant. Clearly, a decision which affects
all residential ratepayers in a similar manner is a
decision which affects the public generally. On the other
hand, a decision to place 40 or 50 ratepayers in a special
rate category or tier cannot be said to affect the public
generally. Other factors include the shared
characteristics of the group, i.e. whether they are a
heterogenous group with varying interests and needs such as
a residential neighborhood or whether they are an industry
or professional group such as oil companies or doctors.

In view of the relatively large number and diversity of
commercial and industrial ratepayers, we concluded that
they could be considered a significant segment of the
public generally for the purposes of conflicts
analysis.6/ Thus Commissioner Grew can generally vote in
all rate matters. However, if a particular decision
potentially involves singling out a subgroup within this
group, it would be necessary to determine whether this
subgroup was also a significant segment of the public
generally.

In the particular situation presented here, it did not
appear that commercial and industrial users of over 2,500
therms per month constituted a significant segment of the
public generally.7/ It is a very small subgroup

_______________________

6/ We based this on the figures concerning Southern
California Gas Company. We can assume that comparable
statistics would be available for the service areas of
other large utilities. However, in the case of small or
rural-based utilities, this conclusion may not be valid.
In these cases, it would have to be assessed on a
case-by-case basis.

7/ Assuming that most GN2 cutomers use over 2,500
therms per month and that most GN1 customers use under 300
therms per month, I roughly calculated that 929 users
(270-commercial, 659-industrial) at the most use over 2,500
therms or .4% of all industrial/commercial customers.

Steve Weissman
May 13, 1982
Page 7


consisting only of the largest users. Therefore, it was
necessary, and would be necessary in similar future cases,
to determine whether an effect which applied only to this
subgroup was material.

I hope this discussion has been helpful to you. I know
that this analysis often seems elusive, but it is necessary
to use a case-by-case analysis of all of the facts in this
area. It is difficult to formulate rules which can be
applied in all situations. I remain available to assist
you at your request.

You also raised some procedural questions concerning
disqualification. If an official has a disqualifying
financial interest, he or she must abstain from all
participation from the point at which the official knows or
has reason to know he or she has a financial interest.
Prohibited participation in a decision for a disqualified
official includes participating or testifying at hearings,
presiding at a meeting where the matter is discussed,
discussion with other officials, negotiations, and public
or private comments on the matter which are intended to
influence the decision.

If Commissioner Grew is ever disqualified from
participating in a matter, you would not have to disqualify
yourself also based on her financial interest. Ms. Grew
could not attempt to participate in or influence the
decision through you, but you could fully participate
otherwise as you normally do.

If you would like to discuss this letter, please feel
free to call me at (916) 322-5901.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Diane Maura Fishburn
Counsel
Legal Division

DMF:plh
Enclosure

sections.question: Whether Commissioner Priscilla Grew has a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act, Government Code Sections 87100 et seq., by participating in California Public Utilities Commission rate design proceedings for Southern California Gas Company, given her investment of more than $1,000 in General Motors, which participated in the hearings as a ratepayer and customer of the utility.
sections.conclusion: Commissioner Grew is not prohibited from participating in the pending rate design proceedings involving Southern California Gas Company. The effect on General Motors' rates would be less than 1%, which falls far short of the $100,000 benchmark used for determining materiality of an effect on business entities. Thus, the effect on General Motors is not material within the meaning of Section 87103, and Commissioner Grew's participation is not prohibited. Additionally, even if an effect were material, it would likely fall within the 'public generally' exception since the rate design changes would affect all ratepayers in a similar manner.

sections.question_synthetic: Does Commissioner Grew's investment in General Motors create a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act when she participates in CPUC rate design decisions for Southern California Gas Company, where General Motors is a major customer?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Commissioner Grew may participate in the SoCalGas rate design proceedings because any financial effect on General Motors would be less than 1% of the company's annual gas bill and therefore not material under Section 87103's $100,000 threshold for business entities. The rate design changes would affect all ratepayers similarly, potentially qualifying for the 'public generally' exception.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document contains two letters dated April 30, 1982 and May 13, 1982 (with May 13 appearing to be the formal advice letter No. A-32-077). OCR quality is poor in places with some character recognition errors, but Q&C sections are clearly identifiable. The document includes detailed analysis of materiality standards under 2 Cal. Admin. Code Section 18702 and discusses both the materiality and 'public generally' exceptions. Minor date inconsistencies in OCR (1952 vs 1982), but context indicates 1982 is correct.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Commissioner Priscilla Grew has a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act, Government Code Sections 87100 et seq., by participating in California Public Utilities Commission rate design proceedings for Southern California Gas Company, given her investment of more than $1,000 in General Motors, which participated in the hearings as a ratepayer and customer of the utility.

CONCLUSION: Commissioner Grew is not prohibited from participating in the pending rate design proceedings involving Southern California Gas Company. The effect on General Motors' rates would be less than 1%, which falls far short of the $100,000 benchmark used for determining materiality of an effect on business entities. Thus, the effect on General Motors is not material within the meaning of Section 87103, and Commissioner Grew's participation is not prohibited. Additionally, even if an effect were material, it would likely fall within the 'public generally' exception since the rate design changes would affect all ratepayers in a similar manner.

Rate design proceedings involving Southern California Gas Company are presently pending before the Public Utilities Commission. One of the utility's customers, General Motors, presented oral arguments at the hearings conducted by an Administrative Law Judge on the rate design proposals. Commissioner Grew has an investment of more than $1,000 in General Motors. We concluded that the decisions concerning the utility's rate design could not have a material financial effect on General Motors within the meaning of Sections 87100 and 87103.²/ Accordingly, Commissioner Grew is not prohibited ___________________________ 1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted. 2/ The effect on General Motors' rates as a result of any of the proposals was estimated to be less than 1%. Thus the potential effect on General Motors' revenues falls far short of $100,000, the benchmark used for determining the materiality of an effect on very large business entities such as General Motors. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(1)(A). Steve Weissman April 30, 1982 Page Two from participating in the pending rate design proceedings involving Southern California Gas Company. I also advised you that Commissioner Grew would not be disqualified under the Act from participating in decisions that could materially affect the University of California. Her husband's salary from U.C., even if financed by federal research grants, is excluded from the definition of "income" in the Act since it is salary from a government agency. Section 82030(b)(2). Therefore, U.C. is not a "source of income" to Commissioner Grew within the meaning of Section 87103. I hope to have a more detailed letter to you on this matter finished by the end of next week. In the meantime, if I can be of further assistance, please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901. Very truly yours, [signature] Diane Maura Fishburn Counsel Legal Division DMF:plh State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. embedding.summary: FPPC Advice Letter No. A-32-077 addresses whether Commissioner Priscilla Grew of the California Public Utilities Commission has a conflict of interest in participating in rate design proceedings for Southern California Gas Company, given her investment in General Motors, which testified as an interested party. The FPPC concludes that no disqualifying conflict exists because any financial effect on General Motors would be immaterial (less than 1% of its gas bill, far below the $100,000 materiality threshold) and would affect the public generally in the same manner.
